Dave, from time to time we do hear for an investment like this that they need to think it over. I'm curious, and this is going to be a B2B sale, so in other decisions that are made, you know, like I walked into your showroom and you had a beautiful Oriental rug, how was that decision made to buy that? And so what I'm trying to figure out is do they need to think it over? Do they need to get with a partner and they're just not telling me that? Or is it that sometimes they make quick decisions? So you try to figure out, you know, other decisions that are made, how are those decisions made? So many times, you know, what we hear is that they have to think it over, and maybe there's some things that need to happen, you know, or that you need to process before you can move forward. If after you think it over and I call you tomorrow morning, are you leaning towards yes or no? Have I answered everything that you need to do so that you're making your decision fully? Dave? Perfect, yeah, perfect. Yeah, got it. And then I might also, I might try to do a little bit of a takeaway, or I might try to do a little bit of a let's get the paperwork done. Just, you know, I want to respect your time, I want to respect my time. I don't want to run this over, or I'm going to predate this PO, I'm going to predate this PO to, you know, two days from now or three days from now, right? And let's sign it, let's get the paperwork done, because this sounds like something you want to move forward, you just need to process it. And I promise you, listen, you know, I'll back that PO back to you, you need to initial it one more time for me so you know that I'm not going to process it. I've been in business for 25 years, I'm not going to be doing this, you know, I realize that I just, you know, I can't, and if you have sales reps, you can say, listen, you know, I can't be running all over town, I have three appointments tomorrow, but I really want to get this done. So you want to tie that person up, you want to get them to some level of commitment, whether it's, you know, if you're going to, if you're doing marketing for them, let's get that ad started or, you know, let's let you start talking to my designer so you can get the process rolling and that gets them acclimated to it. So that's, that's my quick thoughts on that. Perfect. Perfect. And another one that I'll give you, and again, I believe I learned this from Tom Hopkins, I believe I learned it from Tom, and it's really beautiful and I love it because it's so subtle. And it says, I need to think it over and you simply say, you know, fantastic, I understand that. And you act like you're closing up everything, right? You just like act like you're accepting that objection. And you look like we're gonna start packing up your shit, right? Start closing up stuff. And then you look at me and say, you know, why are you packing up? By the way, by the way, Nick, just, just for clarification, what is exactly is it that you need to think over? And so what happens is that then a guy will typically give you the real objection. Right. They'll typically give you the real objection and now you can handle it. So you know what, what exactly, but the whole thing is to act like the sales presentation is over. So really, really, really, really powerful technique to get to the objection. Then what you want to do is you want to tie it down to make sure that that is the final objection. So person gives you the final objection or gives you and you say, you know, okay, well, other than that, is there any other reason why you wouldn't go ahead and get this today? And if they say no, and then you can handle that objection, then the sale is closed. I mean, it works beautifully. So that was just, that was just great. So I just kind of went over the next thing, which is the agreement frame. Right. I like that. Yeah. The agreement frame comes from NLP, Neuro Linguistic Programming. It is. Actually, I'm sure it doesn't come from that, but they claimed it. So we'll give NLP credit. Basically what you never want to do is, first of all, you never want to use the word but. The word but negates everything you said before it. And as a guy, we've all had this experience. You're talking to a hot woman and you asked the hot woman out on a date and she says, you know, you know, Dave, I, geez, you're a really nice guy. I really enjoyed my time with you. But, well, you know, we all know as guys, what's coming after that word but, right? We're not getting a date. So when you use the word but, you're telling your prospect you are wrong. So the agreement frame uses the word and. And there's three little phrases. Okay. The first is, if you agree with what the prospect is saying, you can say, I agree, Mr. Prospect and. Okay. Which then continues it. Not I agree, but. Because that means you don't agree. Negate, but negate everything you said before. The other is, I understand. You can always understand the prospect's position. So Mr. Prospect, I understand how you feel. The next one is, so I agree, I understand. The third one is, I can appreciate. You may not agree, you may not understand, but you can appreciate their point of view. Mr. Prospect, I can appreciate why you say that. And. Okay. So that's the agreement frame. I understand, I agree. All right. I understand, I agree, or I can appreciate. And then the word and. And what I like to use, it's an oldie but a goodie, is the feel felt found technique. Now you don't necessarily have to use it, but let me just complete this for you. All right. Feel felt found. So Nick says to me, geez, Dave, that's expensive. Nick, I agree. It is expensive. And many of our happiest customers felt the same way. But, that's when you use but. But here is what they found. I understand how you feel. It is expensive. Many of my happiest customers, clients, patients, felt the same way. But here is what they found. And then you go on to explain the big benefit. Right? So, let's just take a scenario. Let's say Nick, you're going in because you are the fashion play of GKJ. Right? So, you're going in for a facelift. All right? So, I'm a cosmetic surgeon. You're going in for a facelift. And I give you the price. And you say to me, I am the fashion play of GKJ, but that's expensive. I can say to you, I agree, Nick. I understand how you feel. I understand how you feel. It is expensive. In fact, many of my happiest patients felt the same way before they did this. But here is what they found afterward. The results that they got and how they felt about themselves and how it enhanced their career by improving the way that they looked. You see what I'm saying? That's how it would go. It's very, very, very fluid and powerful. And then, by the way, you combine these. Right? You combine these. So, now you could take Nick's fluid, what he called the liquid object. Liquid, yeah. Yeah, liquid, liquid. And you could roll it right into that. Right? Right? So, you can combine these strategies. So that is the agreement frame. Nick, the final one is what is called the straight line method. And let's give credit where credit is due on this one. Sure. The straight line method really comes from Wall Street. It was probably best positioned and talked about with Jordan Bellafont, you know, the Wolf of Wall Street. And basically, it is part of his training. So, he goes out and trains the straight line method. But I grew up in the financial services. That's kind of where I broke into sales. And really what it's set up to do is that you're visualizing a sale from the beginning to the end. And it's a straight line path. So let's use Dave's River analogy because I like that. You're on the river and you're taking the river, you're taking a slope boat down the Mississippi River. Right? But there's going to be rocks along the way. And you're going to, it's not going to stop the river from flowing, but you realize that. And so there's going to be bumps. And your job is just to come over those objections. And you know, that's, if you think about the Wolf of Wall Street, that was a hard, hard closing, hard objection going. So we're not going to get into that. And that's not really our mantra and our way of thinking. But what it is thinking is that all your salespeople are going to come up with the same objections. Right? So Nick could sell one way, Dave could sell another one, but the objections are going to be the same. And the methodology is that you come up with all those objections and you come up with two or three viable rebuttals to those objections. So it might be that I need to think it over. Or it might be that the price is too high, or it might be that I don't buy over the phone. You know, for salespeople, for salespeople who don't sell on commodities or stocks, it was I don't buy over the phone and just say, you know, and you were taught, these are the objections to use because there's only going to be X amount of different objections. But really what the objection is at the end of the mind, if you're selling something over the phone, is I don't know who you are and I don't know if I should trust you. So you have to build in the presentation of who you are and why you should trust you. Now they're not doing the GKSE type marketing that we teach you to do that. Or if you're buying a car, it might be, hey, you know, at the end of the day, I think I go down the street and get a better deal from a dealer, right? So you're going to have the straight line to the end of the sale. And so really that's what that is. And it's the entire sales process realizing that there's rocks in the river, those rocks are objections. It doesn't mean the river stops blowing. It doesn't mean that the sale stops moving forward. You just have to know what those are. Write objections, write hand, I like to hand write them. Like Dave said, hand write those objections out and have your people learn them in their scripts. And our script book will provide you with some objections and some responses to those. You have to get the ones that work for you and your business to make them flow. So that's what the straight line is. Perfect. So those steps basically handle objections. So let's see here. Jack says we do have a question on objections. So Jack, let's come on and let's hear what that question is about objections. And let's hopefully answer it. Well it's really kind of a recap I think. I think maybe he missed it or whatever. You got a list of all the interviews. It's like Kurt asking what was the first objection again before the bad experience objection? Well the first one was what I put into the time category. So I need to think about it. I need to wait until the economy approves. I need to think about it. I need to pray about it. If you're in a highly religious area, I need to talk to my spouse. I don't make a major decision with my spouse. So the first set of objections, I kind of lump them into four different categories. The first one was time. And so that's what the objections are. Okay. Perfect. Thanks Jack. All right. So now we have an opening. We've done objections. Now let's go into the close. Closing the sale obviously important. But the truth is that as important as the closes and we talked about this and I think maybe some folks thought we were just brushing over it, but we actually did a lot about closing in the core of this, the one we did with Dan. As important as closes are, they're not as hard or as tricky as people make them out to be. So if you've done everything right up to this point, closing the sale is literally just a natural part of the entire process. It's what the prospect wants you to do because they want what you have to offer. It's not a combative thing. You don't have to get nervous about it. It's just a very, very simple and natural part of the process. With that being said, we did promise that we were going to go over some specifics here and I'm going to give you one from, and again, you're not going to be able to write this down because it's kind of long, but I'm going to give you one that you can take and that you can model. It comes directly out of my psychic sales one to one selling court. I call it the future pacing close. The future pacing close. So this one, since the jewel isn't with us, the jewel of GKIC isn't with us, we'll use him as the person. Jewel is having, and we're going to use an example here which is kind of out there a little bit because I want to prove that it could be used for really anything. So let's say that jewel is having sexual confidence issues, which is probably the truth with the jewel. Wait till he listens to this. It'll kill me. You're lucky you're not in the main office there, Dave. No, I'm in it. He's in Chicago. He's bigger and stronger than me and younger. And so we can use this one anyway. It'll be classic. So what I'm doing is I'm future pacing. And let's say it's six months from now, three weeks from now, two weeks, whatever someone is going to see results. So let's say it's three weeks from now. So I would say, imagine jewels three months from now. You've invested in this product. And now you're tapping into your higher source and getting all the answers you want and having the confidence sexually. And you know that your wife is loving you more because you're taking care of this issue that you're having. How would that make you feel? And they would say, that would make me feel great. Well, that's exactly what this program does for you. So let's get started with the paperwork. Do you want to take option A, option B, option C? That's it. You future pace the outcome that they want to achieve. That's all you're doing. So you can do, let's spell out the paperwork. You can do the alternate of choice close. You can just say, let's move forward. That's it. All you're doing is replaying back to them what they want. Now what we don't have in here is what Jules said that he wants to achieve. So that's what we don't have in here. So that's called the future pace in close. I love that one. And I think we all use it. We just didn't know what it was called. I think that was phenomenal. Because it goes back to the statement that I was making that really, and you brought it up elegantly, that it's not a combative. Sales isn't combative. It shouldn't be a combative situation. You should both be working with the prospect and think of yourselves hand in hand, walking into the same thing. So you're working towards that future together. The master salesmen do that each and every day. I think one of the things that I learned very early on when I was a young cub selling is that the percentage ranges, so it could be anywhere from 50% to 70%, of all sales interactions don't end without the salesperson asking for the order. I found that amazing and I found that staggering. So the one way that I set myself apart was, I don't care how bad I stumble or how bad the presentation is, at the end of it, I'm going to make sure, hey, at the end of the day, is this something that you want? Can we move forward with this? And I think if you train your salespeople, or you train yourself just to, at the end of the conversation, make it natural and make it flow and just ask for the sale, instruct them on how to move forward with the sale if you're selling from the stage, I think you're going to set yourself up and your sales team up for success and you're going to be at a different class than most companies. Just by doing something little, by making sure that each interaction ends with an ask for the order. Totally. And what's funny is, we got from, we're not going to say who it is, right? Somebody watched the initial broadcast that we did when we created the core of this program with Dan. And they immediately sent out an email to their list, which Nick happens to be on their list because Nick's like on everybody's list. And it was kind of slamming us. And it basically said, well, when you come on my call, which is happening next week, we're going to be giving you a ton of closes that you can use. Right. The point is, that's not the point. That's old-fashioned selling guys. The canned freaking closes. It's old-fashioned selling. If you're doing everything right, which we're teaching you to literally, in this step-by-step to do everything right, you don't need 50 closes. You don't need to memorize 50 closes. All right. It's not necessary. And in fact, here's the other thing. Customers, clients, prospects are sophisticated. They know most of these canned closes. Right. They know most of this stuff already. And so when you use a canned thing that they learned themselves, it totally blows everything. You now go from being the consultant to being the pushy sales person. So you don't need a million closes. I'm going to get back to you when I said I'm going to get back to you. If it doesn't make sense, I'm going to tell you specifically how it does. It makes sense. I want you to use this as a learning opportunity. And he's using the same pitch as I'm going to date myself, but as I got in the 1990s, right? You know, that's a long time away. And so once we peel back, and that's what we're trying to teach here, once you peel back that conversation and you have a straight line conversation, you have a conversation that is meant to be on the same side of the table as the person going forward, it just makes it a much better purchase for everybody. And so that's my thought about canned and canned pitches and the sales trainers that are out there doing it. You know, it is what it is and we'll move forward. I think, you know, really, I've grown my businesses with three or four, right? My wife and I enjoy every meal that we have here. Your staff is well, your staff is very well trained. I hear you. I hear you. And let me tell you this. One is I am the owner of RSVP Chicago, so I get appreciated. So every dollar I spend in marketing, and I realize everybody hates buying advertising and marketing, because we'll say it's an investment or it needs to run three to four times before it works or anything of that nature. But the reason why I enjoy the product that I do and the reason why I've been doing it for so long and the reason why it works for so many is I could prove to you that you'll get a return on your investment. Is that something you'd be interested in hearing about? Yeah, can you guarantee it? I could guarantee it, and I could also prove with case studies of past people and we'll walk through that. Yeah, yeah, absolutely. All right, so and then at the end of the day, after I get done talking and it makes sense for us to move forward, is this something that you'd like to do? Is this something I have an upcoming publication? Is it actually going to go out a little bit around Halloween, so I'm closing it at the end of this week? Is that something that you'd look to move forward with today? If it makes sense for you and I could guarantee it and it gives you a good return on your investment? Tell me, if you can guarantee me that I can get more customers in a high ticket size, yeah, and you guarantee it, of course I'd be happy to. Now let me put one caveat on it, because I don't want to get to the end of my presentation. I don't want us to bait and switch each other. Is that fair? The one thing I'm going to ask you to do is for you to be fair. So if we create an ad for you that works, right, I need the offer to be a good offer, right? I need it to be something that the person can't get anywhere else. I'm not asking you to give away the store. I'm not asking you to give away the farm. I realize that I really have margin and I respect your margin. But you can't say just bring this in and get a dollar off, because I know this restaurant, and I know your average check is $100 or $200 depending on the why. So will you work with me? Well that makes sense. Oh yeah, dude, I want this to work, but here's the thing, I don't want to do something that cheapens my image of my restaurant. Fully understand, and I respect that, and I hear that a lot. And as you know, do you know some of the restaurants that work with us? No, I don't. Okay, perfect. Then what I would do is I'd go over the list of people. So I might have testimonials, right? And so I might even have a sheet of all the, I do have a sheet, and my salespeople all work around a sheet of all the different restaurants that go with us. And so I would give that with them. I would talk a little bit about in the presentation, and we could do it, or I could say, you know, I know, you know, Wildfire, you've been doing it with us, Lowry Steakhouse, you know Lowry's of course. Oh yeah. You've been with me for the last 15 years. Yeah, I know Lowry. Yeah, they're like, great guy. He goes in three, they go in three a year. The October one is their favorite, one is they're getting ready for it. And I would do the same thing with three or four others, maybe five others, five other peers or things of that nature. These people all go with us. They're very smart marketers, they're a very smart businessman like yourself. They all track their numbers, and they all retract their return on investment. So at the end of the day, you know, they're not going to do something just to do something. And I don't, I'm not a kind of publication that we just say to get your name out because I hate that type of advertising. And you know, because you don't want to get your name out, you want people to come into your store and spend money. Because you can't really pay your meat vendor by just getting your name out. Just like you can't pay your meat vendor with likes on Facebook though too, right? I mean, right, that makes sense at the end of the day. Yeah, and the truth is I don't even know if that crap's working. Perfect, boom. So Dave, thank you. What we did is, right, when I first came in, his first objection was this wasn't working, right? And I only do Facebook and I only boom, boom, boom, boom, boom. So I'm going to try to tie in and really find out was that just an objection or was he just, you know, does he really, really, really focus on Facebook? Fernando from Denver had a, it's kind of a hire, very specific question. It's about, can you explain the difference between the morning after strategy? It's something used as what we call soft in the beach head or maybe pre sell you has the, as the business consultant or the, whatever, whatever you're doing. So it's used to pre sell you, pre frame you and pre position you at the level that you want to be pre framed, pre sold and pre, uh, and positioned at. Um, and then the morning after is just post sale. So it's two different strategies, both great, both great to utilize, both need to be utilized. But one is a pre and one's a post. Yeah, totally. Um, he will get scripts obviously, but the answer to this guy is he's going to have different scripts and different opening statements. If it's a followup, well, you know, you don't need to, would it be helpful to tell you a little bit about myself and my company and how I got into this business? You know, so those are opening statements that you use. I forget how you did it. You did it so well, but so those are opening, you know, kind of first meeting, because I think we're talking about first meeting and then maybe followup meetings. And so followup meetings, you might say, well, you had an opportunity to review everything. I know that, you know, you were excited about everything that we kind of put on the table. Are you ready to move forward? You know, that could be a little abrupt and abrasive and it might be too fast. You might need to do anything of, you know, I've been thinking, I used to say, hey, listen, on the way in over here today, if I'm doing it face to face, I was thinking about our last meeting. I was thinking about our presentations that we did and I was thinking about all the great answers that you gave me. And I'm very excited about the, you know, the presentation I'm going to put forward or the solution I'm going to put forward for you today. So I might reference the past meetings. I would tell them that we've been thinking about them and it's truthful. I've been thinking about them. I would think about them on the way over and I might talk a little bit about the future state. And then I would, you know, ask to move forward with, you know, kind of presenting my findings or presenting my recommendations. So that would be, in my mind, the difference between the two and how to go about them. Yeah. I couldn't agree more. The only thing that I would do in addition to that is recap the agreement of what we agreed upon before, which is really recapping the benefits that the person can receive, but that's spot on. Okay. So we'll get to some more of your questions in just a little bit, but now let's get into a really cool topic, which is sales mastery. We should probably name this section something different because the name of the course is sales mastery, at least. So I got it. This is advanced sales mastery. Oh, that's tricky. Tricky. How do you do that? I mean, it's scary how I come up with this, isn't it? It is. So this is sales mastery. Let's talk about a really important topic, Kit, on NIC, which is immunity to the word no, because if a salesperson cannot get past this issue, well, they're not going to be successful. So let's talk about immunity to the word no. Yeah. I know it's a small word, but it holds up so many people in life, and we've all been there. But until, and I really think the important thing is we talked a lot about Dr. Maxwell Maltz and Theater in the Mind and all that stuff's important. And I like to use examples via stories. And if you know who Jerry Weintrop is, which is the producer of Ocean's 11, 12, and 3, the remakes with George Clooney and the karate kid and all of that, he has a book out, and we'll get questions about the books. I'll give you the title of the book in this, and you know I'm dead because I've stopped talking. And he also has a, there's a documentary about it, because he's a very, very well-known producer and promoter and all that. And I think it's called His Way because he used to, I think it's called My Way or His Way because he used to be the promoter for Frank Sinatra. So this is a Hollywood promoter and producer. And they were asking him in his documentary, what made you successful or what position you were successful? And he came up, he was the salesperson, he sold clothes, he sold clothes during the army. So he didn't have a problem kind of asking for the word no, asking for the sale. And he said, the number one thing to my success is I had immunity to the word no. I never heard the word no. And he, in his book, tells a story about, his big jump to big money is when Elvis did the tour in the 70s, he was the promoter for that tour. Right, so that was huge money for him. And that positioned him for his future growth. And he told in the book that he literally called the Colonel, which was Elvis's manager, every day for 115 days. On the 116th day, he finally got the guy in the book. And he goes through the story about how he asks him three questions, right? And the Colonel asked, said yes on every question. And he said, Colonel, I'd like to be the promoter for you and Elvis's next tour. And the Colonel said, no. He said, whoa, wait, we had a great conversation. We had three great yeses. How did we get to know so fast? Right? So the Colonel then started breaking out laughter. And then it allowed him to kind of break his pattern, break his objection, and move forward. And I thought, well, it's a great story. We all know who all these people are. But it's a great way. It talks about the three yeses, right? So this is the guy in the 70s talking about stuff that we just talked about today. And it talks about that. And I think we get so tied up with no. It breaks down so many salespeople. We don't want to hear it. That's how they end up getting call reluctance. That's how they end up getting everything. And the problem is, it's just a word. And many times people don't mean no. It's just their new jerk reaction. And I think you mentioned the sales trainer that you were trained by. And Dan just did an interview with him. Tom Hopkins. Tom Hopkins. Thank you. And he said, hey, listen, people have to say no before they say yes. The importance is to realize that. It's just part of the process. They may not even mean it. Many times it's a knee jerk reaction. It's the first thing that happens. I live in the city of Chicago. Go down to the street of the city of Chicago and stand out and see people walk into the train or bus or whatever they're doing. If you give me a dollar, I'll give you $20. I can guarantee you, half the people, that's the best deal you're ever going to get in your life. That's a great return on your investment. But they're all going to say no to you. Because they think there's some string attached to it. Or there's some scam or it's a bad 20 or something of that nature. So realize it's just part of the process. We have to be immune to it. Sandler says, he used to crack up, he'd hire all ex-football players, 250-pound guys. But they'd be so afraid of the word no that the telephone handle was the heaviest thing they've ever picked up on their life and they couldn't pick it up because they were afraid of the word no. So once you get through that, I've been doing this long enough to know. It's like water off my back. You're going to hear no. It's part of the process. It just is. And you just have to become immune to it. I know it's easier said than done and realize that. And I'm not taking this lightly or giving glib statements. I'm using stories to tell. Realize, I mean, we've all been there in life where no paralyzed us. But you have to break through that to move forward. When I really, really wanted to be successful on sales, I knew I had to become immune to the word no. I didn't like it in the beginning. But the more and more I realized it's just part of the process. It's part of the sales process and why people are saying no. I was able to break through. Dave, when you and I were in the green room kind of getting made up before we went on the video taping with Dan, you and I both were talking about, you said, listen, for me to become really good at copywriting and selling is when I broke off on my own and I had to be because I needed to be successful. And I think the same thing for me. I had a lot of success as a salesperson in corporate America. But when I put out my own shingle and I started my own company, the stakes got higher. And Chase Bank didn't want to hear I had a lot of good meetings or I met a lot of nice people. So I had to become immune to the word no. And I had to move forward through the objection and through no to get to yeses. And you don't steamroll over people. If it makes sense for them, everybody agrees it makes sense and you want to move forward and you know in your heart of hearts you believe in your product, it's your duty to ask for that sale. If you know your solution solves a problem that they're struggling with, it's your duty and obligation to ask for that sale. And I believe that. And I always say, hey listen, I got to believe thoroughly in what I sell for me to move forward to ask and I'm going to be successful. I don't believe in what I sell. I am not going to be successful. I just, you know, we had an ex salesperson at GKIC. I was just talking to him the other day. He's going off to Mexico to work for a new company. And you know, he said, you know, I believe in this product just like I believe in GKIC's product because I got it. He was our number one producing salesperson for the longest time. He believed in the product and he felt it was his moral obligation to ask for that order because he knew it was life changing for him. So that's a long, it's a long answer to a short word. The word is no and don't be afraid of it. You have to become immune to it. It's just part of the process. And I think one of the key things that you just said and I cannot emphasize this enough, it's your belief in the product. Does not mean that you have necessarily gotten the results from the product, right? So again, that goes back to the other question the other gentleman asked. Well, what if I don't use the product? I'm not using, doesn't matter. You have to believe with your heart, like with unflinching, unflinching belief that your product or your services delivering a result far greater than the price you're asking for. And the perfect example is me selling magnetic marketing. I sell a ton of magnetic marketing and if you go to the boot camp, I don't even, Nick, I don't even do a pitch for it. If you've ever seen it. I mean, I just, you've got to get this. This is what, I know that this thing works, right? I mean, just listen to me now. I know it works and I believe that every small business owner needs it. Period. And now I'm not saying that that could be your short presentation because that's not always the point. Right, that's not the best right, right, right, right. But the point is I have such a strong belief that that belief, it's unshakable. It's unshakable. Therefore, there's nothing that a prospect would say to me, which would make me doubt that they should have this product. And when you have that kind of belief, immunity of the word no is quite easy. And in fact, let me, I'll rephrase how I actually think about this for the, so for the recording. But when I was selling, someone asked me the other day, actually it was in my platinum meeting. They said, how good of a magician actually were you when you were a magician? I was successful but made a lot of money. That doesn't mean I was really good at it. I said, well, as a kid show magician, I was the best. I'd go up against anybody in the country. I was definitely the best in Atlanta, right? And so, Nick, I had such a strong belief that this was true from results that I had gotten. My attitude, and again, this was not something I would verbalize, obviously, to a prospect who was calling me. But my attitude was, if you hire somebody else, you're effing crazy. You're like making the biggest mistake of your life because you're crazy. And I use, you know, that strong belief. And when you have that belief, getting immunity to the word no is a little bit easier because you're like, what? You possibly be saying no. Obviously, I didn't explain something to you. And I've done that. And I've actually said that. Like, what? You know. So, anyway. So, let's talk a little bit now about mindset. Perfect. And the first thing I want to talk about here is a technique which comes out of psychosobernetics, which is the theater of the mind technique. And I use this a lot. And it's funny. They teach us in NLP, as you can probably tell, I studied a lot of NLP stuff. But it really comes from Dr. Maxwell Maltz in what he calls the theater of the mind. And the technique, I'm going to explain to you step by step. And you have to use it. If you use it and you really do it, it will work for you. The most successful people I know use this strategy. So the first thing that you do is you need to construct a movie theater in your mind. So you really want to construct it in detail. How does the movie theater look? What are the chairs like? All of that. How does it smell? You want to get it? How does it look? How does it feel? Really get a full sensory experience of this. So once you've constructed this movie theater in your mind, you and your imagination go sit in the movie theater. And then the movie starts, so imagine the lights going down. And then you watch yourself on the screen in a movie doing your presentation with a prospect. Now, the way to do this properly is you want it to be a tough prospect. All right? Like I was with Nick. You don't want to make it so there's no objections and it just goes smoothly to the close because the reality is this is probably not how it's going to go. So you want to run the movie. You're the director. And so you run the movie. You hear and see yourself making the presentation. You see your prospect hammering you with objections and you smoothly, just as Nick did, smoothly answer those objections. You really go through the entire sales process into the close. And then you run that movie a few more times. So there's a next step which does come from NLP. Okay? And I'm going to give you the next step which a lot of people don't know, which is add a whole new level of power to it. So you run the movie in your mind. I can tell you if you do this properly and in the beginning it's difficult, especially if you don't visualize well and I'm one of those guys, but you'll get it and it's worth practicing. What happens is when you're actually in the sales presentation, you're going to have a deja vu experience. Right? And because it's going to be like you've already done it. And if you've already done something, then you no longer really fear it. It's no longer a goal. It's something that you've accomplished. And so you can do this in your mind. So when the prospect in real life comes up with the objection, you've already seen yourself handle the objection. You've already seen the whole process work through. So that's the basic. That's not a theater of the mind. Now where the neurolinguistic programming comes in is this. And this adds a whole new level of power to it. Because you then imagine yourself getting up off, standing up out of the chair, walking up to the screen and actually stepping into the screen, into your body on the screen. And so now you are no longer watching the movie, but rather you are now in the movie. So the two states that you're going from, you're going from a disassociated state, a dissociated state to a fully associated state. So you watch it first, and then you walk up to the screen into the movie and play the whole scenario again. Now, instead of you looking yourself up on the screen, you are now watching, you are actually in the movie. And by doing that, you truly internalize the entire process. So that is the description of how to use the theater of mind and actually an advanced version of the theater of the mind. Nick, do you have any questions on that or is there anything I missed on that? And I do this strategy, by the way, almost every time when I have to do a big on-stage sales presentation. This is what I do. Because now when I walk out in front of the audience, I've already done it. I've already done it. So I'm not nervous. I'm more excited. So any comments on that, Nick? Hey, it's funny. I've studied Dr. Malts and I use visualization. I never heard that last connection. So I wrote that down. I was taking notes too, which is the most fun for us is to learn from each other. So that was amazing. And I used to study key sports guys and peak state for that when I was getting for sales and visualization was part of it and always on sales calls when I was on the road, I would on the way there and think about it but also visualize it successful. That specific one and then I would do broader sales things. So I'm a firm, firm, firm fan of visualization, theater of the mind. I'm a big fan of it. I think if you could just go through all the objections in your mind and cover over them. I think it's very, very important. People won't do this. This is what moves people to the top one percenters. You got to do this stuff. And I think we all do it in different ways, but we're all doing the same thing. And I think it's a very important thing. The mind is powerful. Yeah, absolutely. So again, a little advanced, but as we promised you, we promised you advanced material. All right. And we promised you a two hour call, but it looks like a six hour call. That's all right though. That's all right. Rather deliver more value. All right. So Nick, talk about the psychology of closing and call reluctance. We kind of talk about psychology of closing, but what about call reluctance? Not even one of those? Sure. I think it's a terminology that gets thrown around a lot in sales. And I think one is it's prospecting, right? So people don't want to make prospecting calls because they're a fear of people saying no. So a lot of it could be solved quite frankly, and this is going to sound right. Doing a lot of the things that we teach in the pre-sale, so sending out direct mail, focusing on what we teach in magnetic marketing, shocking off packages, those type of things, that makes the person want to receive your call. But it goes back to just the mindset and the immunity for the word no. Because call reluctance is I have to make X amount of dials to cold prospects, and I don't feel like making those calls. This is really what ops a lot of people out of sales. And many times they opt out of sales and they just haven't told you, so they're still in your seat, but they just opt out of sales. Because they just are fearful of calling people to set up the prospect, calling suspects, to move them to prospects, to move them to have a meeting, and then move them to customers. And I think you have to realize that it's just part of marketing, right? So if you were to get a 3% response rate on a direct mail campaign, you would think you were successful, right? But if you get a 3% response rate of putting 100 phone calls into an afternoon, you would think you were a failure. But actually, I always thought that I was a success. So what you want to do just to get over-call reluctances is keep stats, not that sales is a numbers game, but just keep stats, just so you prove to yourself that, hey, this works. If I make X amount of dials, I get X amount of appointments. If you're in a situation where you need to set up appointments versus if your customer is setting up appointments or things of that, one of the things that we talked about in another program that you and I did is maybe if you just have people that are closers, then you just have openers that are working for you that their job is just to pound through phones, calls all day long. We have one of those at GKIC, and then he's opening up cases for people. But that's just another strategy. So it's another mindset technique. If you have to set your own appointments and you have to do your own prospecting, you just have to come to grips with that as part of life. And it's easy to say, but it's just something you have to come to grips with. Now, I'm a firm recommender of doing everything else, and there's good programs out there just like ours of teaching you things you could do so you don't always have to pick up the phone and call people. Though I think it's important to do that, sometimes in this day and age it is tough to get a hold of people. So you do have to do other things like direct mail, social media outreach via LinkedIn and things of that nature, cold emailing, all of that. We have a program and there's other programs that go out there and teach that, but realize it's part of sales. We all struggled with it in the beginning, and the sooner you get through it, the sooner you teach your salespeople to get through it, the easier it is for everybody. Perfect. So let's talk a little bit about something, Nick. You often will tell your team that selling days should be selling days. Sure. What exactly does that mean? Yeah, so I'm a firm believer of this, and this is actually a Dan Sullivan thing, but it's, you know, so we want to give credit where credit is due, but selling days are selling days, right? So you have prospecting days, you have pre-days, you have selling days, you have post-days, and you have free days, right? But selling days are selling days. So when you're selling, you are selling. You've got yourself in a peak state. You've got different things to do that, whether it's music or exercise or whatever it is, but you are focused on selling, you are focused on presenting, you are focused on closing. You don't do other things. You're not worried about all the little miscellaneous trivia things. So, you know, baseball players aren't worrying about picking up their dry cleaning when they're in a pennant race and that's out there, them up for bat. So you've got to focus on, I'm going to hit this out of the park, or I'm going to get a single, or I'm going to do whatever. You know, if you're a football fan like Dave and I are, you know, they're not thinking about anything except for what's going on during that time that they're on their field. And you as a salesperson need to treat yourself that way. I'm a peak performer. I am focused on my objectives. I'm focusing on helping my customers and I'm there to sell. And that means you don't do anything else. You've got to either buffer yourself or have people around you to take care of the miscellaneous things. Now, you can't ignore those because that's called servicing and you need to do a lot of the other miscellaneous things, but you're not worried about those on your selling days. So you want to have a day and it's hopefully two or three days of the week where it's just appointments and you're going from appointment to appointment to appointment and you're in a constant state of readiness and a constant peak state and you're going from sales appointment to sales appointment to sales appointment. Or it might be mornings. I need to be three mornings and then the afternoon is when I do my other miscellaneous or busy work or other work that needs to happen as part of the sales process or part of the prospecting process. So I'm a firm believer that sales days are sales days. I've done that. Once I've learned that, it opened up my eyes to many things and I learned it first from a freedman in insurance sales. I read his book. He was focused on, he had selling days and he had non-selling days and then later on I learned it from other people. So Dave, when you're on stage, you're not worried about anything else, are you? No. A really great analogy also is that of a heart surgeon or the surgeon. Again, as a sales person, whether you're like a pure sales person or you're an entrepreneur, a business owner who sells, you're a sales person. But think of yourself on that same level. There's no reason why you shouldn't be thinking of yourself on that level. So a heart surgeon is, you would hope, 100% focused on doing the surgery on the day he's doing the surgery. And that's what you have to be. You've got to be 100% focused on selling and really selling. So that means meeting with prospect. That doesn't mean prospecting. That means selling, like, making sales. And the more of those days you can put in, the better off you are going to be. Nick, one of the things that you're a real master at is inbound, how to handle inbound phone calls from prospects. Because obviously we get a lot of those at GKIC and you train the GKIC team on how to do that. So can you talk a little bit about inbound calls? Yeah. Inbound calls could be a number of different things. There's two schools of thought. One is take it immediately. Other school of thought is you take it, you frame it with the person and you put them on hold slightly. You know, hey Dave, thanks for calling. I want to get to you. I'm just finishing up with another client. It's going to take me about 30 seconds to get done with him. You know, is that okay? If I put you on hold for 30 seconds and the person normally says yes, put them on hold. Get yourself in the peak state, you know, and then go back to that. And then you say, you kind of walk through the process. So, you know, hey Dave, I appreciate your call today. You know, how can I assist you? You try to figure out their pain, figure out what they're calling for, do a diagnostic if it makes sense during that, and then you kind of move them to the close. But, you know, many people fumble when they're in the middle of doing something, they're putting out a fire and they take a prospect call, an inbound call. And I think that's one of the reasons why we want to talk about this. Because you're not ready yet psychologically to take that call. You may think you are, and you may think that you could turn on a diamond. Only the pros can, right? You know, snap your finger and turn. So, you really have to acknowledge the call or just wait a second to take the call to get yourself in the state of mind. Because it goes back to selling day. That's a selling opportunity. You don't want to waste it. So, say something was happening on a delivery if you owned a company and the delivery didn't go well and you're answering an email from the delivery company and you're in the middle of a heated exchange and you don't want to take an inbound call. You know what I mean? But you don't want to lose that inbound call. So, you need to reframe yourself. Really quick reframe yourself, take it and then get ready. I'm a firm believer if you're inbound and you're down, get on a headset. It has to be on a headset. I'm on a headset here. I've walked around my office. You've got to move around to get yourself in the state where you're taking an inbound call. You've got to keep your energy up. I mean, we've been on this for two and a half hours so it's easy to let your energy down. If the force is to get your energy up and you know, I'm here. Everybody does different psychological things and physiological things. Figure out those things and they can be quick. Snap, you've heard me snap my finger a couple times. That's something that I do to reframe myself, right? Because I got a glass door and people were going by but I got to sign this as I'm recording. So, you know, I want to be focused on this for you guys and for the people future that are listening to this. So, reframe yourself, take the call. It's a sales opportunity. It's probably the best sales opportunity you're ever going to get in your life. So, don't waste it by coming off of an exchange, a heated exchange, and taking that call because you're not going to be ready for it and it's not fair to the prospect of the client either. So, that's... Yeah, you're totally right. And one of the things that I do because, again, quite honestly, I'm beat from a two-day meeting. But it's one of the things that I do and I know you do it too, as I have a headset on right now and I walk around. So, I'll actually get up and walk around and I bet you anything, if you go back and listen to this recording, you can tell the points where I'm walking around and I'm moving and I'm grooving and when I'm sitting on my ass, right? So, again, that's just... Maybe that won't work for you but for me, it certainly does. So, you've got to figure out those strategies for yourself. But get into a peak state because if you're not into a peak state, you're not going to make the sale. Final thing, Nick, about this, then we're going to go over my system for closing... My consultant closed for closing a high ticket sale over the telephone with one call. Interruption. What about interruptions? What do you have to say about that? Don't get them. Don't take them. Right. So, are you talking about... So, if you're interrupting, you've got to create an environment around you where you are selling. So, if it's your environment and you're on the phone, we do phone sales here. In past lives, I've done phone sales as well as face-to-face sales. You've got to create an environment around you when you know you just can't deal with interruptions. That's why I'm a firm believer of not taking sales calls on your car phone, right? It's not fair to the people around you but don't do that. It is so hard to get in front of prospects in this day and age. Take every opportunity and set yourself up for success. Be a pro. Yeah, be a pro. There are people. Can you believe that there's people that take calls on their cell phone while they're driving? Sales calls, it's insane. Yeah, but they do. I've heard guys on sales calls in the bathroom in the airport. I'm like, oh my gosh, come on. I'm embarrassed. Take a couple minutes. Just buy a day pass to go into the United Lounge. You've got to have margin in your business so it just makes sense. I just think it goes back to be a pro. Set the table for success. Set yourself up for success. You can't be interrupted. You've got to be in peak state. You've got to do everything you can do but don't allow interruptions because it's easy. It's just easy and you can get lazy. I have fallen into that trap until you realize what's happening. You don't want to waste the sales, especially in today's environment. Yeah, totally. All right. The final thing that we're going to discuss on this call before we go to Q&A is what we promised you is my system that I used to use when closing. I actually taught this at GKIC. The system that I used to use to close selling... Let me start over here. You know what I'm going to do? I'm going to stand up here. I'm going to stand up and move around, buddy. Easy for you to say. The system I use to close high ticket sales over the telephone. I was closing. That guy said between $15,000 and my highest close was $40,000 over the phone in one call. You might be thinking your BS meter is maybe going off saying no way. Well, no way unless you know the entire system. In one of the manuals that you're going to get from us as part of this program, it has the outline of the script I use to close sales. But most people would say, oh, that's all I need. Well, it's not. You need the other parts of the system because there was a lot more to it than just getting the person on the phone and following that script. So let me lay this out for you step by step, precisely how it worked with the example of exactly how I did it. Now, I'm not going to modify the example. You need to figure out for yourself how you can modify the example. I'm going to explain to you exactly how I did it. So first you need to know what I was selling. I was selling, done for you, tele-seminars and webinars because I'm pretty good at putting those things together, right? Including the entire marketing package. And by the way, don't call GKIC. Don't email me. I don't do this anymore. So that's what I was selling, complete marketing package and the webinar or tele-seminar done for you. So that's what I was selling between $15,000 and $40,000 depending on what you wanted me to do. Depending on how much, which package you took. So the way I started this was I sold it via a tele-seminar. I shouldn't say I sold it via a tele-seminar. I started the sales process via a tele-seminar. So I would send out an email talking about this tele-seminar that I was going to teach about how to do tele-seminars, how to do effective tele-seminars and webinars, and people would opt in. So let's just break that down right there. So out of my customer list, I had, I narrowed it down to a group of people. I said this email, these emails out to everyone promoting the actual call, but the only people who registered, those are the people who were interested, right? So I did do a call for everybody. I narrowed my prospect group to people who were interested. That's the first thing. So people would register for this tele-seminar that I was doing. On the tele-seminar, I would follow my script and how I put everything together to sell. But what I would do on this is I would teach in detail, in detail how I put together tele-seminars and webinars and everything that you need to do. So remember, I was not trying to sell a course on doing tele-seminars. I was just selling done-for-use services on doing tele-seminars. So by giving my entire process out, right, somebody could take and do it themselves. I didn't care because I was going for a bigger ticket sale. So I didn't hold anything back. That's very, very different than if you're, when I was trying to sell a course on tele-seminars. I wouldn't give out everything I knew. But on this one, I gave out everything. I didn't get templates or anything like that, but they got exactly what to do. At the very end of that, I would say now, I know there's some folks on here who are wondering right now, geez, this sounds complicated. How much would it be for you to do this for me? And then, so that was my transition to the close. Because guess what? Some people were thinking that exact thing. And then I would tell them my package is ranging from this to this. Now, why did I do that? Well, because I was further qualifying my prospect. If my prospect couldn't afford $15,000 or whatever the package was, then I don't want to waste my time talking to them, right? And then I would say, but by the way, I can only accept two clients a month, which was true because it was me and Jack doing this. So I really could only accept two clients a month. So that was adding the urgency factor. But here comes the key piece. I would say, if you want to apply, if you get this language now, if you want to apply to try to get one of these plots, here's where to go. You've got to fill out this questionnaire. So the psychology on this is now I've taken it from, I'm pitching to their applying to work with me. It totally changes the dynamics of the sales process. So people would go and apply. They would fill out an application. I would also say on the phone, after you apply, I'll review all applications. And you will get one of two emails. So let me just break that down for you. You're going to get one of two emails. One of the things that you want to do, especially if someone is in a business where they don't, if you're in a business where people don't know what the process is, one of the strongest things that you can do is explain to them exactly what's going to happen. So they feel totally at ease. So they're not wondering about anything. So one of two things will happen after you fill out this application. Excuse me, you'll get one of two emails. One will say, I think what you have sounds interesting, and I need to set up a follow-up call to interview you. The other will say, I'm sorry, I don't feel like right now I can help you with your project. By the way, if you get that email, it doesn't mean necessarily that I don't believe in your project. It just means that I don't believe that I'm the person to help you at this time. Okay, so if you look what I just did in just that 20 seconds of copy, and it was copy because it was written down, in that 20 seconds of copy, and what I said, I have done takeaway selling. So now the person is hoping to get an interview. First of all, they're hoping now that I accept their application and that they get the email that says, hey, we're going to have a follow-up call. Okay, so then, by the way, I would really do this, because people would have really lousy projects and I really only want to work on projects that I thought would work. So I would send out the two emails. The email would go out for the person that I was going to go forward with. That email would say, hey, listen, I read over your application, your project looks good. I want to talk to you to see if we're a good fit to work together. And I'm going to be doing my calls on such and such a date. Here's what's going to happen. My personal assistant, Michelle, is going to call you. She's going to call you one time. And she will leave a message if you're not there. But if you don't call her back within 24 hours, that's it. All right, we're not going to follow up and chase you, because I've got a whole bunch of people I need to talk to. So, you know, when I talk about this, I just realize how beautiful this actually is. Getting excited about it again. By the way, this is, I know it sounds cliche, but if you can use this, what I'm sharing with you is worth ten times what you paid for sales mastery on Leash. All right, so then Michelle would call. And when she got the, by the way, if she left a message, almost every woman would call within 24 hours, right? Because they've been told to call within 24 hours. They were playing by my rules. I was congruent all the way through with this. Everything I said, we need to talk, we wanted two emails, we need to apply, all of that. When she would talk, she would set up the call, and then she would add, and kind of like she's on their side, she would say, no, let me just give you a little tip here. Mr. D, and she would use Mr. and I don't like people to call me Mr. by the way, I like Dave, but it was important for this positioning here. Mr. D, first of all, make sure you call exactly on time. If you're a few minutes late, he's not going to take your call. Also, he hates small talk. He hates small talk, and he's going to have a bunch of these calls back to back, so you want to get right to the point. So she's acting like she's giving them some inside information. What she's really doing is positioning me. And setting up, you better get your ass on that phone on time. So, almost everybody, not everybody, not 100%, but almost everybody called exactly on time. So I didn't have to sit around and wait for people to call me. Also, it made them a little uneasy, a little nervous. By the way, that's positive. Tension, a little bit of tension is good. And then I was much friendlier on the phone, and she made it seem like I was. Now, I had their application in front of me, telling me the application told me everything I would need to know to sell them. It told me why they want to do this, what's preventing them from doing it themselves, what do they want to accomplish, what their pain points were. So now I would just go through the sales process that Nick and I went through, but I didn't have to find anything out because they told me ahead of time what they wanted. Then I would explain to them if I decided to take them on as a client, here's exactly what would happen. Here's how we would do it. Here's the plan, and here's your personalized plan. And you could do it yourself if you wanted after this. And I would go through the personalized plan, and then inevitably at the end of the presentation, I didn't even close, and then it has to. The person would say, well, are you going to take me on as a client? What do I have to do? I want to be one of the two. How can I be one of the two? Almost every single time. Well, how much exactly would it be for you to do this for me? So there wasn't, but then my close was, again, the statistic, I wouldn't close the sale then. Because I said to them, and this was true, by the way, if they did sound good, I would say, this sounds really good. I really believe I can help you with this. In fact, I know I can help you accomplish this and get this done. However, I have four, five, how many other calls I have, and I have to go through those first. I'm going to pick the top two people who I feel I can really help the most. So tomorrow you're going to get one of two emails. One of the emails is saying congratulations, and I'm taking you on. Or the other one will be, I'm sorry, I just don't think right now I've chosen two other people, so on and so forth. If you are accepted, then I would tell them what they need to do. You need to PayPal me half of the payment to get started. There'll be agreement. You've got to get it back to me. You need to get it back to me within 24 hours. It was basically the same as we did earlier when we were doing the phone call, right, when Michelle was setting up the phone call. And then that's exactly what happened. And I would say, and Jack could correct me if I'm wrong, I would say 90%, maybe even a little bit more, of the people who went through this process who I sent the agreement to, bought because why wouldn't they were kind of begging me to take them on as a client? Right, begging me to give me money. And this process was so strong that, and Jack will absolutely verify this because he knows exactly the person who I'm talking about. I went through the entire process and at the end I thought that the woman was kind of a flake. And I didn't want to work with her. And so I said, right at the end of that, right in the end of the call, I said, you know what, let's say her name was Mary, I said, you know what, Mary? I just don't think I'm the person right now to help you with this. Okay, so, and then Mary would say, well, no, no, I want you to do this. And I think, well, you know, Mary, I think your project sounds promising. I just don't think I'm the right guy to help you. And then she said to me, no, no, you don't understand, I have the money. It's not a money problem. I said, no, I understand it's not a money problem. I just don't think I'm the right person. No, you don't, she started getting pissed at me. So now she's kind of yelling at me to give me $20,000, right? And so I got out of that and then she emailed me this big long thing trying to repitch me on why I should do her project. So that is the power of that system. And out of integrity, here's the thing, this system is so powerful on a psychological standpoint, from a psychological standpoint, you have to be in total integrity, meaning you have to reject people who you truly can't help, especially if it's a high ticket sale. Now, let me tell you something. There were times where I needed that $20,000 that month, right? And it's hard to say, no, I'm not going to. But you have to, unless the entire process will eventually break down and you're not going to get referrals and you're going to feel like really crappy about yourself. So that is the entire system laid out for you exactly like I did it. And, Jack, if you want to come on, and if I missed anything, or Nick, if you have any questions about it, I'm happy to answer them. But that's the system that I use. Well done. I was impressed by it. I could see how that works. You know, I think it's a great system. Everybody could take either all of it or part of it that's on this call or that buys this product and figure out a way to utilize it in their sales process because there's no better positioning than the way you just set up. So I highly recommend everybody trying to figure out how they could get it to work for their business. Because it works for everybody, but it's just how do I implement it in my business? Yeah, just use pieces of it. I mean, just use pieces of it. But the whole thing is you need to remain consistent and congruent throughout the entire process. You know, the interesting thing I thought, and it goes back to why it fits so well in this category, David, it just dawned on me because I've heard you present this before. I really have. But each time I learn from it, but it fits so well in the sales mastery section because we're talking about mindset. And you said something that I think was a throwaway sign, but I know you well enough that I know you mean it in your heart of hearts is you said, listen, you have to have integrity in this process. You actually have to say to people, listen, no, I can't work with you. It's not going to work. Because that's what gave you the ability to say no, even when you knew you needed to say yes for financial reasons, but you still have integrity in the process. And I think that's what gets in the way of people sometimes. When you think about mastery, they may not be selling something that they truly believe in, or they may not have integrity in their process or have really come to that grip. So that really is a high level. I don't think it was a throwaway comment because you don't do throwaway comments, but it was a comment that I wanted to at least call out. So everybody picked that up because like us, they've been on this call for three hours too. So they may be missing it. But it really fits into the sales mastery aspect of things and why it's important to have that much integrity in your process that you won't vary from it. Because then in the future, when you said this is my process, you're not wavering and people could read that if you're wavering. Yeah, totally. One is he needs to have his designers to have either on their iPads or Flipbook, wherever they're doing is different types of end projects. Because sometimes we could see people and we see their personality, i.e. if you're doing it for my wife, you would have a picture of my wife, and then the end projects. And they have to show obviously different styles. And talk a little bit about, if you're using it for my wife, this is Mrs. Luise, you want to use her full name. Tell a little bit of background on her, and tell her how you got to that design, and then show pictures of that. So, she loved travel, she was into cooking, she was from French, yadda yadda yadda. These are all the important things about her, and then this is the design that we have. And you want each of those to show different and tell a different story, because each person is different and each design approach is different. And I think that also has to come across in your sales and your sales presentation. Listen, we're not a cookie cutter, and that's maybe why we're a little bit more expensive. But we'll be afraid of price. We're not cookie cutter. We approach each thing with a complete diagnostic interview. Here's the 14 points or the 22 points or the 22 questionnaire, so that way we customize your design specifically for your personality, and your lifestyle, and your family, or however the appropriate thing is. And I think you really want to stress that. Every conversation, every sales thing, everything in the presentation has to be that way. So the designer needs to, you have to script them, and script them in practice, and have them practice among themselves, and oversee them in the showroom while they're doing that. But don't be afraid of price. Explain why your price is higher. And these are the reasons why. Differential yourself in the sales process. Listen, you got guys that come in, they're coming in with dirty trucks, they're drawing a picture on the back of the purchase order. They're going to tell you to sign. They may not show up. The second thing is, a lot of this could be handled if you're doing the GKIC style marketing to get your prospects. So that objection could really be handled way in advance through advanced material that they get before that they meet with the designer. And if you're really high-end, well, then you've got to be taking them through a process, not necessarily the same process that I laid out when I was selling high-end services, but rather a process where they get something before they even come in. A lot of things happen, a lot of bad things happen when you're not totally congruent. Because if you're not totally congruent and you're just like everybody else, or you're perceived as being just like everybody else, then price is an issue. Because even the richest guy on the planet doesn't want to pay extra if he doesn't have to pay extra. So you've got to think about your entire process. Jack, do we have another question?
